chapters.title: Family ties shape how a person meets the world
chapters.text:
People say there is always something you might tell your psychotherapist, even with a happy childhood. It is hard to call Tara Schuster family broken. They lived an ordinary life in their Los Angeles home, and they had a tradition called Family Sunday. And yet she did not feel cared for. She felt abandoned, like their house, which needed a deep clean and a major renovation.

No one packed her gym clothes for school. She never had a dentist of her own, and she had no idea that meat should be eaten with salad. She grew up listening to her parents argue, and they eventually divorced. She split from her sister Diana when their father left with Tara, and Diana stayed with their mother. She kept hearing her mother judge her intelligence and her body. Once she caught her father smoking pot and felt ashamed that he used drugs. Schuster was scared by the thought that her only parent was high. She asked him not to smoke, at least not around her, but he refused. Out of despair, she tried his remedy. She found his stash and smoked. That is how weed became her self-medication.

A person who never learned to care for themselves as a child may grow up unable to see their own value.

She worked hard to get into Brown University and escape that suffocating atmosphere. But the foundation of her self-hatred was already set. She spent her undergraduate years following her father relaxing habit and feeling ashamed after her mother humiliating words. When a playwriting teacher praised her work, Schuster took it as mockery. She had sex only in the dark and hid her anxiety in a cloud of smoke. After graduation, she felt lost and had no idea what to do next. In desperation, Schuster moved to New York with her boyfriend.

Their relationship was toxic, full of cheating and jealousy. When they split up, Schuster felt abandoned again, as if something were wrong with her and everyone left her for a reason. As usual, she dulled the pain with weed and chased approval at her first adult job, The Daily Show with Jon Stewart, all the while crying quietly in her cubicle. Then her twenty-fifth birthday came, and with it came suicidal thoughts. She realized enough was enough. She chose to become the best version of herself at her worst moment.

chapters.title: Writing and physical activity help the brain release anxious thoughts
chapters.text:
Changing your life sounds great, but where do you start? Schuster was so tangled up in her emotional damage that it was hard to see things from above. On a friend Isabel recommendation, she bought The Artist Way by Julia Cameron, a summary is available on the Headway app, which promised to surface and treat her emotional wounds. The most useful tool she took from that book was the practice of Morning Pages.

Every morning she wrote three pages about everything that was on her mind. Fears, worries, beliefs, and dreams flowed onto the paper, letting her face the past and picture the future. The practice freed her mind from disturbing thoughts, turning them into calm fantasies. Still, the deep traumas were untouched. Schuster needed to quit smoking weed to find and heal them, and she did.

Without self-medication, Schuster faced intense anxiety after a recent breakup and had to step up her therapy. She wore a bracelet on her wrist to snap against herself whenever disturbing thoughts came. That built a neural link between thought and pain, and unpleasant thinking went down, but it was not enough.

She asked her friend Julia for advice. Julia had always struck her as calm. Julia surprised her with a confession. She had her own periods of worry and anxiety, but she had a remedy that cleared the negative thoughts: running. Schuster had hated her body and exercise since school, but she bought a sports bra and sneakers and went for her first jog. Her friend had not been wrong. It helped.

Physical activity took the load off Schuster overloaded brain, and there was a side benefit. Every workout was a small win. She counted the days without panic attacks, and her sweaty T-shirt stood for her honest effort. It may look like a small thing to someone, but for the woman who, the day before, had done nothing with her life, it meant a lot. Only Forrest Gump might run with more enthusiasm than Schuster.

You can come up with your own moving meditation if you need help reducing worry. Pilates, boxing, or jumping on the office stairs all count. Any attempt is worth it.

The first step often feels the hardest, but the challenge has to keep going once you have started.

chapters.title: The strength of positive thinking
chapters.text:
Dropping bad habits is not easy, especially when those habits live in how you think. Schuster had grown so used to the idea that something was wrong with her that she saw the world through dark glasses. A few rituals helped her notice and value the good things inside and around her.

The first is positive psychology and a gratitude practice. Try to find a few things you are grateful for and write them down each day. At first, Schuster did not want to dig deep and wrote about espresso and new candles. Soon, she was surprised to find strangers smiles, her cozy apartment, her friends, and even her family on the list. How many good things are around you?

Happiness is built from many small moments of joy. It is important to notice and value them.

Another Schuster method for better mental health was writing thank-you cards. How does it work? Almost like magic. By sending a card to someone, you send your positive feeling out into the world and create a circle of good energy. Had a rough meeting with your ex? Write a card to a friend thanking them for a great dinner, or to your yoga teacher for a calming class.

Then she decided to face her inner critic. Her mother had nurtured this Frenemy Within that always lived close by and insulted her. It checked every idea Schuster had and handed down its verdict: it is as wrong as you are. Schuster wrote a letter to her Frenemy Within, crushing every claim that she was an impostor and would never reach her goal, and confirming that there was no such thing as too late for her. She was talented, she already had accomplishments, and she would not stop on her way to new aims.

Schuster also reviewed her social circle and made three lists. A support team of people who accepted her without any ifs or buts. Role models who motivated her. And people who put her down.

Sorting them out helped her pick the right person for feedback in different situations. By taking small steps, Tara Schuster learned to cherish and care for herself.

A useful note. DOSE is short for the main neurotransmitters of happiness. Dopamine is released when you reach a goal. Oxytocin is tied to trust in a relationship. Serotonin can come from feeling proud of what you have done. And endorphin levels rise through hard exercise, stretching, laughter, and crying.

chapters.title: The key to self-care is recognizing your own value
chapters.text:
One of Schuster most meaningful projects may have been the idea board. Even though she was skeptical of vision boards, she built a map inspired by the book A Way In: A Map to the High Holy Holidays, drawn from the synagogue. She wanted to immerse herself in who she was and to like that person (you may remember the famous moment in the film Bridget Jones Diary when Mark Darcy tells Bridget those words). Schuster was ready to know herself and to love this person. She made three new lists and pinned them to her doors.

Things that raised my self-esteem. My principles. True things about me.

These lists helped her define her essence and the path to the best version of herself. Following the same idea, you can start with affirmations like I am productive, I am a good friend, or I am stylish. It is best to be honest with yourself.

It is hard to love yourself if you do not know who you are.

When people do not value themselves, they often do not allow themselves to buy what they want or need. Schuster childhood had tangled her relationship with money. Her parents had never treated money as a base for a stable life. They could forget to pay bills and still take vacations in Hawaii. Her father took out a loan for her private school and always complained about the debt. As an adult, Schuster had no problem with expensive brunches but hesitated to buy new jeans or extra-soft toilet paper. By studying self-care, she began paying bills on time and stopped denying herself basics and small joys. Both scheduling a gynecologist appointment and buying her favorite lilies contributed to her well-being.

Next, she learned to value her time. Old Schuster was always busy trying to be efficient at work, showing up at every party she was invited to, and regularly spending time with friends. It looked like a full life, except for one thing: she had no time for herself. New Tara Schuster started Monday evening by reserving hours for her own things, including reading, binge-watching, wearing a Korean face mask, or simply being alone.

Setting time aside for yourself is a great idea. How about dancing in polka-dot pajamas on a Wednesday after work?

chapters.title: It is essential to feel at home in your body and to take care of it
chapters.text:
Tara Schuster spent two and a half years getting her mind in order. Back in Los Angeles, she worked on building rituals to care for her body. Unlike New York, that sunny city calls on you to take over the nearest hill right away. One day, she conquered that hill and dozens of other routes. Still, she had to love her body and trust in her strength.

Schuster first paid attention to her looks, inspired by her flatmate beauty habits. She bought good-quality cosmetics and tried to picture Cleopatra (her role model) getting-ready rituals: listening to her favorite song, lighting incense, facing herself in the mirror, putting on perfume and accessories, and saying thank you to the universe.

It looked like a morning routine worthy of a goddess, and indeed it was.

She also discovered the pleasure of taking a bath. Like Chandler Bing, she could enjoy soothing water for hours. The ritual began after a trip to a Japanese hot spring. On top of that, soaking naked in the pool with women of all ages freed her from shame about her body. It was time to make it healthy and strong.

After giving up weed and going to the gym, Schuster felt she had a healthy lifestyle down, until she heard about the recommended weekly alcohol limit. She drank much more than that. The news pushed her to set drinking rules to cut down on unpleasant consequences for her body and reputation. The key is to know your limit. One more cocktail will likely do more harm than good. She also took up better nutrition. She listened to her body needs, learned about water balance, and added vegetables and healthy snacks to her diet.

A person and their surroundings shape each other. Build a place that lifts you up.

Schuster wanted to be in tune with her space and cleared out all the clutter from her apartment. It was ready for guests at any time, with no piles of unneeded things, dirty dishes, or old stains on the rug. There was one place, though, where she did not invite visitors: her home office. Schuster treated her new inner state with care and built a small private corner where she could be herself, with her lists, affirmations, pain, and dreams, the place where she could run her own world.

chapters.title: Build the family you choose
chapters.text:
Building a relationship is never simple. Finding your people is not the same as finding the perfect perfume, but it is possible. The key is knowing who you want around you. Schuster treasured her friends, who filled her life with meaning. They were her real family, since she had no close relatives, and she made sure to show how much she loved them. She saw them as a collection of gems, each with their own spark. Kindness. Self-confidence. Self-awareness. Curiosity.

She also wanted friends who did not carry the same emotional scars she did. Some people look for loyalty. Others want a friend with a sense of humor. It is fine to reach out to people who can give you something you lack.

Spend time with people who inspire you to be your best self.

Schuster romantic life was harder and felt a bit like Groundhog Day. She dated men with a similar profile. They looked great at first, with good education and jobs, but they carried their own emotional baggage and did not know how to care for themselves or for her. The more broken or cold the partner, the more she was drawn in, since she had grown up feeling abandoned. She tried to save them, earn their love, and build a picture of happiness on Instagram, but it did not work.

Schuster reflected on her pattern, spotted the destructive script, and said a quiet thank you in her head to all her past partners for the lessons. Then she knew what she had to avoid in her next relationship. No more self-absorbed partners, no more abusers, and no more wrong-fit guys. She gave herself permission to be picky, no matter how long it took to find the right person.

For better or worse, people cannot choose their relatives. She understood that her parents would never become the mom and dad she had imagined. It was painful, but she learned not to take their attitude personally. They had hurt her, but not on purpose. They had not known how to love and care for their kids. That realization brought huge relief. Schuster stopped blaming herself for not deserving someone else love. Each person had their own issues, and those issues had nothing to do with her.

Above all, Nora Ephron once wrote, be the heroine of your life, not the victim.

chapters.title: In closing
chapters.text:
Everyone has a unique life story. You do not remember the moment, but it piles up from your very first breath. You go through good times and bad times, but you always matter more than anything that happens to you.

The first human setting, the family, often shapes us the most and sets the patterns of our thinking. Sadly, childhood is not always the happiest stretch of life and can carry painful experiences. The words and actions of parents become the first items we pack into our life luggage. Year after year, we live, have fun and feel down, love and break up, succeed and fail, filling our suitcases with experiences. One day, the bag gets so heavy that we cannot lift it. We need a strong will and a big wish to open it and swap out the old, useless things for new, good ones.

Tara Schuster managed to care for herself and sort out her emotional baggage. It took a while to check every pocket and change habits she had built over years, but in the end, her bag grew lighter. She filled her new life with what she wanted and put her own needs first. She learned to live, not just to survive, left the past in the past, and focused on her present. Thanks to simple rituals, Schuster enjoys every moment of her life and wishes the same for her readers.

Try this. Taiichi Ohno at Toyota Motor Corporation developed the Five Whys technique to explore the cause-and-effect links behind a particular problem. For instance, if you want to buy a cake for yourself, try answering the question Why five times in a row. It can surface hidden reasons for your wishes and help you discover your inner needs.

Worth reading from start to finish?
